title: House of Wisdom flourishes in Baghdad
summary: By the 820s and 830s the Abbasid capital of Baghdad had become the largest translation and research centre the medieval world had ever seen. Under the caliph al-Ma'mun, teams of Christian, Muslim, Jewish and Zoroastrian scholars turned the philosophy, mathematics, astronomy and medicine of Greece, Persia and India into Arabic, and used them to launch new sciences of their own. Their working library, called Bayt al-Hikma, the House of Wisdom, became a byword for the confident cosmopolitanism of the Islamic golden age.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
Baghdad had been founded in 762 by the caliph al-Mansur on the west bank of the Tigris as Madinat al-Salam, the City of Peace, a perfectly circular administrative capital for the new Abbasid dynasty. Within two generations the round city was spilling suburbs across both banks of the river; by the reign of Harun al-Rashid, from 786 to 809, Baghdad was already probably the largest city in the world outside China. Its wealth came from the fertile Sawad of southern Iraq, the trans-Eurasian trade routes converging on the Persian Gulf, and the tribute of an empire stretching from the Atlas to the Indus.

Abbasid political culture was self-consciously cosmopolitan. Persian bureaucrats staffed the diwans; the caliph's guard was drawn increasingly from Central Asian slave soldiers; the great Barmakid family of viziers, whose ancestor had been a Buddhist priest at Balkh, blended Iranian and Islamic learning. The dynasty had also inherited a translation impulse older than itself. Under the last Umayyads and the first Abbasids, Syriac Christian physicians from the Persian medical school at Gundeshapur, most famously the Bukhtishu family, had already begun to render Greek scientific texts into Arabic for court patrons. Al-Mansur was said to have secured Ptolemy's Almagest and Euclid's Elements as diplomatic gifts from Byzantine emperors.

The institution known in later sources as Bayt al-Hikma probably began as al-Mansur's private library of translated Persian works, expanded under Harun al-Rashid into a more substantial khizanat al-hikma, or treasury of wisdom, in the palace complex. Some modern historians, notably Dimitri Gutas, have argued that no formal institution of this name ever existed and that Bayt al-Hikma was simply the caliphal library, its supposed academic character an anachronistic projection by later chroniclers. Others, including George Saliba and Jim al-Khalili, maintain that under al-Ma'mun something recognisably institutional did emerge, whatever its precise organisational form. What is not in dispute is the extraordinary scale of the intellectual work being done in Baghdad from the late eighth century onwards.

Al-Ma'mun, who became sole caliph in 813 after a devastating civil war with his brother al-Amin, gave the enterprise a decisive push. According to the tenth-century bookseller Ibn al-Nadim, al-Ma'mun once dreamed that Aristotle appeared to him and, on being asked what was good, answered: whatever conforms to reason, whatever the law approves, and the opinion of the majority. Whether or not the dream is historical, its citation by later scholars captured al-Ma'mun's reputation as a philosopher-king who put the resources of a superpower behind rational inquiry. He is said to have paid translators the weight of their finished books in gold.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
The translation movement in Baghdad reached full stride in the 820s and 830s. Its greatest single figure was the Nestorian Christian physician Hunayn ibn Ishaq al-Ibadi, born in Hira around 809. Fluent in Syriac, Arabic and Greek, and having studied medicine in Alexandria and Byzantium, Hunayn returned to Baghdad in the 820s and became al-Ma'mun's chief translator. Working with his son Ishaq and his nephew Hubaysh, he produced Arabic versions of most of Galen's medical works and of Hippocrates, as well as of Plato's Republic, Aristotle's Categories and De Anima, and much else, adopting a rigorous method of collating multiple Greek manuscripts before settling on a text. He described his procedure in a surviving Risala or epistle to his patron Ali ibn Yahya, one of the most valuable documents of medieval scholarship.

Roughly contemporary were the three Banu Musa brothers, sons of a former highwayman turned court astronomer, whose vast fortunes financed translation expeditions to Byzantium in search of manuscripts. They wrote a celebrated Book of Ingenious Devices describing more than a hundred mechanical automata and hydraulic devices, and sponsored the mathematician Thabit ibn Qurra, a Sabian from Harran, who translated Apollonius's Conics and much of Archimedes into Arabic and made original contributions to number theory. In the same milieu the Persian mathematician Muhammad ibn Musa al-Khwarizmi, appointed by al-Ma'mun to work on astronomical tables, wrote his Kitab al-Jabr wa al-Muqabala, from whose title our word algebra descends, and a treatise on Indian numerals that would eventually give Europe its number system. His name, Latinised as Algoritmi, became our word algorithm.

The philosopher Abu Yusuf al-Kindi, the first great Muslim philosopher and known as the Philosopher of the Arabs, worked in the same court, supervising translations of Aristotle and writing his own treatises on metaphysics, optics, cryptology, music and cosmology. Al-Ma'mun also commissioned practical projects: astronomical observatories at Shammasiya near Baghdad and at Mount Qasiyun above Damascus, staffed by teams that measured the length of a degree of the earth's meridian in the deserts of Sinjar and Palmyra to confirm the size of the globe, and a great world map, no longer surviving, produced by al-Khwarizmi and his colleagues. The chief librarian and administrator of these enterprises, in the traditional account, was Salm, a Zoroastrian convert to Islam who served as sahib bayt al-hikma.

Beyond translation the House of Wisdom hosted the great intellectual quarrels of the age. Al-Ma'mun himself imposed as state doctrine the rationalist Mu'tazilite theology, which held the Qur'an to be a created rather than an eternal text; a mihna or inquisition compelled judges and jurists to profess this view, most famously excepting the traditionist Ahmad ibn Hanbal, who was flogged for his refusal. The tension between rationalist and traditionalist strands of Islamic learning that shaped later Muslim thought was already fully visible in the debates of Baghdad in the 830s.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
The intense activity of al-Ma'mun's decade could not be sustained at the same intensity by his successors, but the infrastructure it created endured for two centuries. His half-brother al-Mu'tasim, who moved the capital to Samarra in 836 to house his growing Turkish slave army, continued to patronise scholars. His successors al-Wathiq and al-Mutawakkil supported Hunayn ibn Ishaq and other translators, though al-Mutawakkil's rejection of the Mu'tazilite mihna in 848 restored the more traditionalist Sunni orthodoxy that would gradually dominate Baghdad. The translation movement continued through the tenth century under Christian scholars such as Yahya ibn Adi, and reached a further peak in the eleventh century under Ibn Sina, or Avicenna, in Iran.

The scientific consequences were vast. Al-Khwarizmi's algebra founded a mathematical discipline of its own; his Indian numerals passed via Latin translations at Toledo in the twelfth century into the medieval European curriculum. Ptolemy's Almagest, translated in Baghdad and later corrected by Muslim astronomers, remained the working textbook of European astronomy until Copernicus. Hunayn's Galen, revised again by later Arabic and Latin translators, was the basis of medical instruction at Salerno, Montpellier and Bologna. Even the Latin philosophical revival of the twelfth and thirteenth centuries, from Adelard of Bath to Thomas Aquinas, depended on Arabic recensions of Aristotle that reached the west through Andalusi Muslim and Jewish scholars, above all Averroes and Maimonides.

Baghdad's institutional infrastructure of libraries, hospitals and observatories was much imitated. Similar institutions were founded at Cairo under the Fatimids in the tenth century, at Cordoba under the Umayyads, at Isfahan under the Buyids and Seljuqs, and later at Maragha under the Mongol Ilkhanids. The idea that a court, or a religious foundation, might sponsor systematic collective research, though not without precedent in the ancient world, was reasserted in Baghdad on a scale not matched again until the European universities of the twelfth century.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The end of the Bayt al-Hikma is bound up with the end of Abbasid Baghdad. In February 1258 the Mongol general Hulagu, on his westward march, stormed and sacked the city; the caliph al-Musta'sim was killed, and the great libraries along the Tigris, according to Ibn Khaldun and later chroniclers, were thrown into the river to make bridges of books. The colours of the water, it was said, ran black with ink for days. The Persian polymath Nasir al-Din al-Tusi, then a Mongol councillor, is credited with rescuing tens of thousands of volumes and transferring them to his own new observatory at Maragha. Whatever precisely was lost, and modern historians treat the number sometimes cited of a million volumes with caution, an institutional continuity that ran back to al-Mansur was broken.

The cultural memory of Baghdad's golden age proved indestructible. In modern Arab and broader Muslim historiography, the House of Wisdom stands as the emblem of Islam's contribution to world science, invoked from the reformist writings of Muhammad Abduh in the nineteenth century to the founding manifestos of research universities across the twentieth-century Middle East. Iraqi initiatives named after it were established in Baghdad in the 1990s and 2000s. Even in western popular writing, from Jim al-Khalili's Pathfinders to museum exhibitions in London, New York and Doha, the House of Wisdom has become a shorthand for the moment when Baghdad was the intellectual capital of the world.

The historiographical debate over just what Bayt al-Hikma was, a formal academy, a working library, or an idealised label attached later to a diffuse translation movement, remains open. But the substance of what happened in Baghdad in the second and third quarters of the ninth century is not in doubt. In perhaps three generations, teams of scholars there absorbed the surviving scientific inheritance of antiquity, added significant original work of their own, and transmitted the whole to the Latin west and the Persianate east. It is difficult to think of another episode in cultural history that did so much in so short a time.

worldContext.text: The Carolingian empire of Louis the Pious, Charlemagne's son, was drifting toward the civil wars among his own sons that would tear it apart at the Treaty of Verdun in 843. Viking raids on Ireland and the coasts of Francia intensified through the 830s; Dublin was founded as a Norse trading base around 841. In Byzantium the emperor Theophilos, an Iconoclast and an admirer of Islamic court culture, corresponded with al-Ma'mun and built palaces at Constantinople in explicit imitation of Abbasid models.

The Tang dynasty, under Emperor Wenzong, was struggling with the growing power of eunuch officials and the Sweet Dew Incident of 835, an abortive coup against them that left hundreds of officials dead. Trade with the Islamic world continued through Guangzhou, where a large community of Arab and Persian merchants had established itself. In Japan the Heian court under Emperor Ninmyō presided over the beginnings of a distinctive courtly culture that would flower a century later in the Tale of Genji.

Northern India remained divided in the triangular contest of Pala Bengal, Pratihara Kannauj and Rashtrakuta Deccan. The Pala king Devapala continued to patronise Nalanda and Vikramashila. In the south the Pandyas ruled Madurai and the Cholas were beginning their rise. Sanskrit science flourished in the mathematical tradition of Brahmagupta, whose seventh-century work had already reached Baghdad and had been translated into Arabic under al-Mansur. Islamic Sindh was governed from Multan under a semi-independent Habbarid dynasty.

The Aghlabid emirs of Ifriqiya were completing the conquest of Byzantine Sicily, taking Palermo in 831 and pushing on toward Syracuse. Their capital at Kairouan supported its own translation and legal schools, and their coinage circulated across the western Mediterranean. In sub-Saharan Africa the Wagadu kingdom of Ghana continued to dominate the gold trade of the Sahel, and Christian Nubia under the Makurian king Zakharias sent embassies to Baghdad.

In the Maya lowlands the Terminal Classic collapse was well under way: within a few decades Tikal, Palenque, Copan and Yaxchilan would be abandoned in the drought and warfare of the ninth century. The Toltec city of Tula was rising in central Mexico. On the northern Peruvian coast the Chimu were beginning to organise around the emerging capital of Chan Chan, and the highland Wari empire had already fragmented into successor polities.

figures.name: Al-Ma'mun
figures.role: Abbasid caliph from 813 to 833; chief patron of the translation movement and organiser of Baghdad's astronomical observatories

figures.name: Hunayn ibn Ishaq
figures.role: Nestorian Christian physician and translator; produced Arabic versions of most of Galen and much of Hippocrates and Plato

figures.name: Muhammad ibn Musa al-Khwarizmi
figures.role: Persian mathematician and astronomer; wrote the founding treatises of algebra and introduced Indian numerals into Arabic mathematics

figures.name: Banu Musa brothers
figures.role: Muhammad, Ahmad and al-Hasan, sons of Musa ibn Shakir; wealthy patrons of translation and authors of the Book of Ingenious Devices

figures.name: Al-Kindi
figures.role: First great Muslim philosopher; oversaw translations of Aristotle for al-Ma'mun and wrote widely on philosophy, optics and cryptography

figures.name: Thabit ibn Qurra
figures.role: Sabian mathematician from Harran; translated Apollonius and Archimedes into Arabic and made original contributions to geometry and number theory

images.caption: Scholars in an Abbasid library, from a 1237 copy of the Maqamat of al-Hariri illustrated by Yahya ibn Mahmud al-Wasiti. Bibliotheque Nationale de France, Paris.
images.credit: Yahya ibn Mahmud al-Wasiti, 1237, via Wikimedia Commons

sources.title: Foundation of the House of Wisdom in Baghdad
